_all_text: Calcite calcite CaCO3 Most stable polymorph of CaCO3 at surface conditions. Mn substitution produces pink mangano-calcite; Co produces cobaltoan calcite (pink-purple). Fe and Mg substitutions common in sedimentary and metamorphic settings. Complete solid solution to rhodochrosite (MnCO3) via kutnahorite. Calcite's extraordinary morphological diversity makes it among the most recognizable minerals despite no single defining habit. Clear Iceland spar shows remarkable double refraction — images appear doubled. Dogtooth clusters are among the most architecturally beautiful mineral specimens. Iceland spar's double refraction fascinated early scientists — it was central to Huygens' (1690) wave theory of light and was used in early polarimetry. Some researchers have proposed Viking navigators used Iceland spar to locate the sun in cloudy Arctic skies, though evidence remains debated. Calcite is the mineral of civilization — every major stone-building culture from Egypt to Rome to the Aztecs used it. clarity structure foundation purity amplification Building stone (limestone, marble) since antiquity Iceland spar used in Nicol prisms for polarized light research Marble carving (Greek and Roman sculpture, Italian Renaissance) Agricultural lime (soil amendment) Cement and concrete (Portland cement) Chalk for writing Acid neutralization (water treatment, mine drainage) Glass making (limestone flux) Steel manufacturing flux Statuettes and carvings (alabaster calcite) CaCO3 calcite crystal Iceland spar dogtooth calcite nailhead calcite mangano calcite limestone mineral marble mineral double refraction effervescent mineral carbonate calcium trigonal cleavage-mineral common cave-mineral building-stone high-priority morphological-diversity crystalline-foundation cave-architecture morphological-diversity ghost-clarity civilization-stone colorless white gray
